items.evidence: Speaker is a Tougaloo summer-1963 EVS volunteer in the Tougaloo Social Science Lab orbit. Whisper 'Barinsky' is the canonical phonetic Whisper failure pattern for Ernst Borinski, the German-born Jewish refugee sociologist who founded the Tougaloo Social Science Lab and ran the famous interracial Social Science Forum. Speaker explicitly says she 'regretted that I did not meet Dr. Borinski' (raw Whisper 'Dr. Barinsky'), 'He was apparently on vacation' — this 1963 absence is consistent with Borinski's biography. Memphis Norman heading the Social Science Lab that summer further triangulates: the Social Science Lab IS Borinski's institutional creation. No other surname in the Tougaloo sociological-faculty population fits both the phonetic shape ('Bar-' opening, '-inski' Slavic ending) AND the institutional context. Conservative bias preserved — this is a high-confidence canonical match, not a speculative replacement.

Greenville MS context is dispositive. Speaker is a SNCC/COFO Freedom Summer volunteer in Greenville and explicitly describes 'Dorothy Jones worked for the Hodding Carter family' (raw Whisper renders the proper name 'Hottie'/'Hotting'/'Heidi' across multiple instances) and the pool-swimming anecdote where the volunteers were invited to swim in the Carter pool. The Hodding Carter family of Greenville MS owned the Delta Democrat-Times — Hodding Carter II (1907-1972) was the Pulitzer-winning white moderate editor; Hodding Carter III (b. 1935) was his son, later Loyalist Democrats co-founder and Carter administration State Dept spokesman. In summer 1964 both were alive and in Greenville. Charles Cobb's separate Southern Mississippi oral history corroborates the pool incident. Phonetic Whisper failure on the unusual first name 'Hodding' is well-attested cross-corpus (cf. Guyot #76.61 'Harding Carter'). Confirmed.

Speaker describes 'Spottswood W. Robinson III, the head of the NAACP, was speaking [at a mass meeting in Jackson]. They sat us on the stage.' Robinson (1916-1998) was the canonical NAACP LDF attorney who, with Thurgood Marshall and others, argued in Brown v. Board; later DC Circuit federal judge (1966-1992, chief judge 1981-86). In summer 1963 he was an NAACP LDF attorney engaged in voting-rights litigation in Mississippi. Whisper's drop of the second 't' in 'Spottswood' is a documented recurring pattern (catalog L-ext entry). Speaker's institutional description ('head of the NAACP') is slightly imprecise — Robinson was not NAACP president in 1963 (Roy Wilkins was) but was a senior LDF attorney; the speaker's casual collapse is normal oral-history slippage and does not undermine the surname identification. Confirmed.

Speaker explicitly says 'my first research I think was at UNC in Anne Romaine's oral interviews' (raw Whisper 'Anne Romain'). Anne Romaine (1942-1995) was the canonical SNCC/Highlander-affiliated folk musician and oral historian whose 1966-67 interviews with MFDP figures form a major UNC Southern Historical Collection holding (the Anne Romaine Papers, #4488). The interviewer Emilye Crosby is herself with the UNC Southern Oral History Program — context aligns perfectly. Whisper drop of final 'e' from 'Romaine' is a documented recurring pattern (catalog E-ext). Speaker says these interviews are foundational to her MFDP convention-challenge research. Confirmed.

Speaker is recounting the 1964 Atlantic City MFDP credentials challenge. She explicitly says 'after she finished writing the brief for Joseph L. Rauh Jr.' (raw 'Joe Rao'), and later 'because Joseph L. Rauh Jr. decided that they... he didn't have the numbers. Aaron Henry said they had 10. Joseph L. Rauh Jr says they had, I think he said, I think, I think he had eight.' These are quoted recollections of Rauh's canonical role: UAW general counsel; ADA co-founder; MFDP lead legal counsel at Atlantic City who negotiated the two-seat compromise. The Whisper rendering 'Joe Rao' is a phonetic compression collapsing 'Joseph L. Rauh' to a two-syllable approximation. No other person in this institutional position (MFDP legal counsel, Atlantic City 1964) fits. Confirmed.

items.new_candidate: Jerry and Lamar Britton (provisional; surname spelling local, not externally verified)
items.evidence: Speaker explicitly says 'The three of us lived with Jerry and Lamar Britton' (raw Whisper 'Britten') and recounts that they 'owned a drugstore at the corner of North and Nelson' in Greenville's Black north end. This is local-historical detail, not a nationally canonical figure. The William Alexander Percy Memorial Library oral-history collection (Greenville MS) and Daisy Bates 1978 interviews referenced by the speaker would be the authoritative source for the exact surname spelling, but 'Britton' vs 'Britten' is not externally adjudicable from the available transcript content alone — both spellings are real English surnames. Original-candidate spelling 'Britton' is retained as the most-likely canonical form (matching the catalog 'Britton' choice in Pass 1 #79.52 and Pass 2 #79.P2.68), but flagged as local-confidence (medium tier preserved) rather than canonical-confidence. Narrowed: surname identification is correct (the Greenville Black drugstore-owner host family), spelling provisional pending Percy Library/Daisy Bates corroboration.
Same evidence as #79.30 — this is the Pass 2 reinforcement of the Pass 1 finding. Confirmed.
Same evidence as #79.13 — Pass 2 reinforcement. Confirmed.

Tougaloo College in Tougaloo MS is a canonical HBCU in the project ground-truth corpus (civil_rights_facts.json line 791: 'Historically Black private liberal arts college in Tougaloo Mississippi founded 1869, central site of the Mississippi civil rights movement of the 1960s', aliases include 'Tougaloo'). Speaker is a documented summer-1963 EVS volunteer at Tougaloo who lived and worked on the campus and learned about the movement through the Tougaloo Social Science Lab. The Whisper variants 'Tugulu' and 'Tugelow' are the canonical phonetic Whisper failures for 'Tougaloo' (a Choctaw place name with non-obvious English orthography). Multiple instances throughout the transcript all refer to the same institution. Confirmed.
Same evidence as #79.P2.38 — recurring Whisper variant of the canonical HBCU Tougaloo College. Confirmed.
Same evidence as #79.44 — Pass 2 reinforcement of MFDP Atlantic City legal counsel Joseph L. Rauh Jr. Confirmed.

The raw transcript captures the speaker's self-correction explicitly: 'Muriel was working in Cherokee with... Isakwena. She went to... You need a black one.' [paraphrased context: 'I think, at least for part of that summer, Muriel was working in Issaquena (or Sharkey) County with... Issaquena. She went to...'] The speaker briefly mis-speaks 'Cherokee' (likely a momentary slip — the Cherokee Nation is in NC/OK, not MS) before self-correcting to 'Issaquena.' Muriel Tillinghast's documented SNCC field assignment in summer 1964 was Issaquena County MS (Mayersville, the county seat), where she replaced or worked alongside George Rawick / Charles Cobb in the COFO Issaquena-Sharkey-Washington tri-county project. Sharkey County is adjacent and was sometimes paired in the field-organizing literature, but the dominant documented designation for Tillinghast specifically is Issaquena. Narrowed to 'Issaquena County' alone (dropping the 'or Sharkey' uncertainty). Whisper preserved the speaker's verbal slip but did not flag the self-correction structurally — that is a separate catalog-F annotation matter, not a resolution question.

items.whisper: Howdy on her... Hotting Carter / Hottie Carter
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.29 — Pass 2 reinforcement including the pool-swimming anecdote that triangulates the Carter family. Confirmed.

Speaker explicitly says 'I've talked to Hodding Carter about it. And Charles Cobb referred to it in his interview at Southern Mississippi.' In the present-tense ('I've talked to'), the Hodding Carter the speaker has spoken with about Freedom Summer in retirement-era oral history is Hodding Carter III (b. 1935), since Hodding Carter II died in 1972. Charles Cobb's Southern Mississippi oral history corroborates the pool incident. Confirmed as Hodding Carter III specifically for this instance (as distinct from the broader II/III family attribution in #79.29 and #79.P2.63 where the speaker is referring to the family that owned the pool, which would have been the household of Hodding II in 1964).

items.whisper: Mrs. Britten / Jerry and Lamar Britten
items.original_candidate: Mr. and Mrs. Jerry Britton / Mr. and Mrs. Lamar Britton
items.new_candidate: Jerry and Lamar Britton (host family; surname spelling provisional)
items.evidence: Same provisional-confidence basis as #79.52. The Pass 2 expansion to 'Mr. and Mrs. Jerry Britton / Mr. and Mrs. Lamar Britton' is one possible parsing of 'Jerry and Lamar Britton' (i.e., two married couples), but the transcript context ('The three of us lived with Jerry and Lamar Britton. And they had a large brick air conditioned house. And Valerie and I had a double bed room on the first floor.') reads more naturally as a single couple — Jerry (husband) and Lamar (wife), or vice versa — than as two couples cohabiting in a single house with one shared 'Mrs. Britten lay on the couch in the den.' The transcript also says 'They owned a drugstore at the corner of North and Nelson. And they both worked in the store. And Mrs. Britten lay on the couch in the den' — the singular 'Mrs. Britten' (one person) and 'they both worked in the store' (one couple) confirm this is one married couple, not two. Narrowed: the host family is Jerry and Lamar Britton (one married couple), not two couples; surname spelling provisional pending Percy Library corroboration.

items.whisper: the latest committee for civil rights under law
items.original_candidate: the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
items.new_candidate: the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
items.evidence: The Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law was founded June 1963 at the request of President John F. Kennedy as a national legal organization to mobilize private-sector lawyers in support of civil rights. The Whisper rendering 'the latest committee for civil rights under law' is a documented homophone substitution ('latest' for 'Lawyers'') — phonetically the syllabic shape /ˈleɪ.təst/ vs /ˈlɔɪ.ərz/ is close in casual speech. The named-construction match on the trailing phrase 'committee for civil rights under law' is canonical and unique — no other organization shares this exact name. Speaker is discussing 1964 MFDP legal-support organizing where the Lawyers' Committee was active. Confirmed.

Morgan, Lewis & Bockius LLP is a canonical Philadelphia-founded (1873) major US law firm with substantial DC presence in the 1970s-80s. The Whisper 'Bacchius' for 'Bockius' is a documented phonetic substitution (the unusual surname 'Bockius' /ˈbɒk.i.əs/ gets mis-heard as the more-familiar classical Greek name 'Bacchius'). Speaker's biographical context — she went to law school after Freedom Summer, then practiced ~10 years at this firm in government contracts — fits the firm's DC government-contracts practice. The name-triplet 'Morgan Lewis and [X]' uniquely identifies this firm; no other major firm uses the 'Morgan' + 'Lewis' two-name prefix. Confirmed.

Walter Reuther (1907-1970) was the canonical president of the United Auto Workers (UAW) 1946-1970. The Whisper drop of the silent 'e' in 'Reuther' (a German-origin surname /ˈruː.θər/) producing 'Ruther' is a documented phonetic-orthographic failure. Speaker's context is dispositive: 'Walter Ruther, who arrived on Tuesday in the middle of the night. And he worked with the Mondale subcommittee to come up with the [compromise].' This is the canonical Reuther role at the August 1964 Atlantic City convention: LBJ summoned Reuther mid-week to apply UAW labor-movement pressure to broker the MFDP two-seat compromise via Hubert Humphrey and the Mondale-chaired credentials subcommittee. Confirmed.

items.whisper: Hottie Carter / Hotting Carter / Heidi Carter
items.evidence: Recurring Whisper variant cluster of the canonical Hodding Carter family of Greenville MS. Same evidence as #79.29, #79.P2.63, #79.P2.64. Confirmed.

Gov. John Bowden Connally Jr. (1917-1993) was the canonical Democratic Governor of Texas (1963-1969), LBJ's close political ally, and the surviving wounded passenger in JFK's Dallas motorcade on November 22, 1963. At the 1964 Atlantic City Democratic National Convention, Connally led the Texas delegation and was one of the Southern governors LBJ deployed to pressure MFDP delegates against the credentials challenge. The Whisper 'Connolly' (Irish-origin spelling) for 'Connally' (Texan/American spelling) is an orthographic confusion — the two spellings are common English-language variants. Speaker's 'Governor John Connolly of Texas' in 1964 convention context uniquely identifies John Connally. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Chairman (David) Lawrence of the Credentials Committee
items.new_candidate: Chairman David L. Lawrence of the Credentials Committee
items.evidence: David Leo Lawrence (1889-1966) was the canonical former Democratic Governor of Pennsylvania (1959-1963) and Mayor of Pittsburgh (1946-1959), appointed by LBJ as Chairman of the 1964 Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee — the very body that adjudicated the MFDP challenge. Speaker's phrase 'Chairman Lawrence of the Committee' in the dispositive August 1964 Atlantic City credentials-committee context is unambiguously David L. Lawrence. No other contemporaneous figure named Lawrence held this institutional role. Confirmed.

William Moses Kunstler (1919-1995) was the canonical movement civil-rights and radical-left lawyer, founding partner of the Center for Constitutional Rights (with Arthur Kinoy and others, 1966), and counsel in many Freedom Summer / MFDP / post-1964 movement legal cases. Speaker says 'Bill Consler and Arthur Canoei would become counsel' — the pairing with Arthur Kinoy is dispositive: Kunstler + Kinoy were a famous legal-partnership pairing at CCR. Whisper 'Consler' for 'Kunstler' is a recurring cross-corpus pattern (cross-ref Guyot #76.51 'Kansler', catalog C-ext). Confirmed.

Arthur Kinoy (1920-2003) was the canonical movement civil-rights and radical-left lawyer, Kunstler's legal partner, co-founder of the Center for Constitutional Rights, Rutgers Law professor, and counsel in many MFDP and Freedom Summer cases including the Dombrowski v. Pfister First Amendment landmark. Whisper 'Canoei' for 'Kinoy' (and the cross-corpus Guyot #76.52 'Kanaui') is a recurring severe Whisper garble of an unusual surname — the same garble pattern appears in multiple oral histories. Pairing with Kunstler (#79.P2.107) in the same sentence is dispositive. Confirmed.

items.whisper: Hiding Carter family / Hotting Carter / Heidi Carter
items.evidence: Recurring Whisper variant cluster of the canonical Hodding Carter family of Greenville MS. Same evidence as #79.29, #79.P2.63, #79.P2.64, #79.P2.101. Confirmed.

The 1964 Atlantic City credentials-committee compromise offered the MFDP exactly two at-large seats (not state-delegation seats) — this 'two-seat' offer is the canonical historical phrase in all MFDP literature (Branch, Dittmer, Payne, Hamer). The MFDP rejected it ('We didn't come all this way for no two seats' — Fannie Lou Hamer's apocryphal but widely-circulated rejection). Whisper homophone 'to seat' / 'two seat' is documented cross-corpus (catalog O-ext, cross-ref Guyot #76 same pattern). Speaker explicitly references 'the credentials committee voted the symbolic two-seat offer' — context dispositive. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Whisper recurring homophone in interview-opening template
items.new_candidate: Catalog A pattern reinforcement: 'world history' Whisper homophone for 'oral history' in interview-opening templates
items.evidence: The interview opens with 'we have the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill' (corrected) — raw Whisper renders 'world history' for 'oral history'. The phonemic confusion /wɜːrld/ vs /ɔːrəl/ is small in rapid speech. The Civil Rights History Project interviewer template uses this phrasing across many interviews — confirmed cross-corpus pattern documented in the existing catalog A row. This is a catalog-extension annotation, not a per-row correction. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Severe Whisper omission of 'e' in UAW president's surname
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Walter Ruther' Whisper rendering of canonical UAW president Walter Reuther
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.91. This is a catalog-extension annotation for a canonical-figure surname-spelling Whisper failure that recurs (catalog L-ext already documents). Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Severe Whisper garble of canonical major DC/Philadelphia law firm name
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Morgan Lewis and Bacchius' Whisper rendering of canonical law firm Morgan, Lewis & Bockius
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.88. Catalog-extension annotation for a canonical-institution name Whisper failure. Confirmed.

items.whisper: "the latest committee for civil rights under law" -> "Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law"
items.original_candidate: Severe Whisper homophone of canonical 1963-founded civil-rights legal organization
items.new_candidate: Catalog B pattern: 'latest committee for civil rights under law' Whisper rendering of Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.80. Catalog-extension annotation for a canonical civil-rights legal organization. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Recurring Whisper rendering of canonical movement lawyer
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Bill Consler' Whisper rendering of canonical movement lawyer William Kunstler (cross-corpus with Guyot #76.51 'Kansler')
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.107. Catalog-extension annotation reinforcing the existing cross-corpus Kunstler-variants pattern. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Severe Whisper garble; cross-corpus with Guyot #76.52 ('Kanaui')
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Arthur Canoei' / 'Canoa' Whisper rendering of canonical movement lawyer Arthur Kinoy (cross-corpus with Guyot #76.52 'Kanaui')
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.108. Catalog-extension annotation reinforcing the cross-corpus Kinoy-variants pattern. Confirmed.

items.new_candidate: Catalog E pattern: 'Anne Romain' Whisper rendering of canonical SNCC oral historian Anne Romaine
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.39. Catalog-extension annotation reinforcing the existing recurring-drop-of-final-'e' pattern. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Whisper drops the second 't' in canonical NAACP LDF attorney's first name
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Spotswood Robinson' Whisper drop of second 't' in canonical NAACP LDF attorney Spottswood W. Robinson III
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.30 and #79.P2.19. Catalog-extension annotation. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Whisper preserves speaker's 'Cherokee' error but does not catch the self-correction structurally
items.new_candidate: Catalog F geographic pattern: Whisper preserves speaker's verbal slip 'Cherokee' but does not flag the self-correction to 'Issaquena' (Issaquena County MS, Mayersville seat, canonical SNCC field territory)
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.59. Narrowed to Issaquena alone (not 'Issaquena or Sharkey') per the dispositive transcript context. This is a catalog-extension annotation about a Whisper structural-failure pattern (failure to detect speaker self-corrections), not about which specific county is correct. Both the Whisper-pattern annotation AND the Issaquena-county identification are confirmed. Narrowed.

items.whisper: "to seat" / "to seek" Whisper homophone confusion at MFDP convention
items.new_candidate: Catalog B-ext pattern reinforcement: 'to seat' / 'to seek' Whisper homophone confusion at 1964 MFDP Atlantic City credentials challenge (cross-corpus with Guyot #76.P3.13; add Anderson Todd #79 to Example entries column)
items.evidence: Same evidence as #79.P2.141. Catalog-extension annotation reinforcing the existing cross-corpus pattern. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: First-name garble of canonical SNCC + Citizen Action founder
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: 'Heidi Booth' Whisper rendering of canonical SNCC volunteer + Citizen Action / Midwest Academy founder Heather Booth
items.evidence: Heather Booth (b. 1945, nee Tobis) was a canonical 1964 Freedom Summer volunteer, founder of the Jane Collective (1969), and later founder of the Citizen Action / Midwest Academy organizer-training infrastructure (1973). Speaker mentions 'I remember Heather Booth was there [at the Freedom on My Mind premiere ~1994-95] and she was saying hello to Senator Paul Simon' — the casual present-tense reference to a fellow Freedom Summer veteran in mid-1990s DC civil-rights film context uniquely identifies Heather Booth. Whisper 'Heidi' for 'Heather' is a phonetic first-name garble. Confirmed.

items.original_candidate: Recurring Whisper renderings of canonical Hodding Carter II/III; cross-corpus with Guyot #76.61 ('Harding Carter')
items.new_candidate: Catalog C pattern: Whisper variant cluster ('Heidi' / 'Hotting' / 'Hottie' / 'Hiding' / 'Howdy on her') for canonical Hodding Carter II/III of Greenville MS (cross-corpus with Guyot #76.61 'Harding Carter')
items.evidence: Aggregated catalog-extension annotation for the multi-variant Whisper cluster documented in #79.29, #79.P2.63, #79.P2.64, #79.P2.101, #79.P2.112. Cross-corpus reinforcement with Guyot #76.61. Confirmed.

items.whisper: "Mississippi students union, the high school students" -> "Mississippi Student Union"
items.original_candidate: Recurring Whisper compression of canonical MFDP youth organization name
items.new_candidate: Catalog B pattern: 'Mississippi students union' Whisper rendering of canonical MFDP-affiliated Mississippi Student Union (MSU) high-school youth organization
items.evidence: The Mississippi Student Union (MSU) was the canonical 1964 COFO/MFDP-affiliated statewide Black high-school student organization, organized during Freedom Summer to mobilize Black Mississippi high schoolers in civil-rights work parallel to the SNCC adult organizing. Speaker explicitly says 'this was instigated by the local chapter of the Mississippi Student Union, the high school students' — context dispositive. Confirmed.
